# Inference {#sec-inference}

```{r .common, include = FALSE}
source("_common.R")
```

This chapter is a reference for the inference machinery in fect. Read it when you have to defend a specific (`vartype`, `ci.method`) choice in a paper, or when you want to understand why log-scale and ratio estimands ship `bca` as the default.

The chapter is organized around three design choices that compose into the inference output:

1. `vartype` --- *where the bootstrap distribution comes from* (unit-level cluster bootstrap, jackknife, parametric).
2. `ci.method` --- *how a CI is constructed from that distribution* (basic, percentile, bc, bca, normal).
3. `para.error` --- *which residual-error model the parametric path uses* (auto, ar, empirical, wild). Sub-option of `vartype = "parametric"`; silently ignored when `vartype` is `"bootstrap"` or `"jackknife"`.

For each cell of the resulting matrix there is a corresponding $p$-value via test inversion, and a minimum number of bootstrap replicates `nboots` recommended in the classic literature. We summarize all of this and document the empirical coverage of each cell on a clean DGP. Below, we use $\hat\theta$ to represent a point estimate.  

## The 3 × 5 inference matrix

A `fect()` fit with `se = TRUE` populates a $T \times N \times B$ array `fit$eff.boot` (or, for `vartype = "jackknife"`, a $T \times (N-1) \times N$ array). Two paths consume this array:

- **`fect()` itself** populates the `fit$est.att` (per-event-time) and `fit$est.avg` (overall) slots at fit time, using the `ci.method` argument supplied to `fect()`. Two values are accepted there: `"normal"` (default; Wald) and `"basic"` (reflected pivot).
- **`estimand()`** is the post-hoc path. It aggregates `fit$eff.boot` into a one-dimensional bootstrap distribution of the requested estimand (`att`, `att.cumu`, `aptt`, `log.att`) and passes that distribution to a CI constructor selected by `ci.method`. Five values are accepted there: `"normal"`, `"basic"`, `"percentile"`, `"bc"`, `"bca"`.

The two surfaces agree byte-equally on the overlap (`"normal"` and `"basic"`) at the avg-level and per-event-time CI sites. The richer 3-method surface (`"percentile"`, `"bc"`, `"bca"`) is the `estimand()`-only path, and is where the per-type defaults (`att.cumu → "basic"`, `aptt → "bca"`, `log.att → "bca"`) live.

**vartype** controls the distribution semantics:

| vartype | construction | distribution centered at | preserves within-unit dependence? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `"bootstrap"` (unit-level) | resample units with replacement, refit per replicate | $\hat\theta$ | yes (whole units stay intact) |
| `"jackknife"` | leave one unit out, refit, repeat for all $N$ units | $\hat\theta$ via Tukey | yes |
| `"parametric"` | simulate errors on `D=0` cells (model selected by `para.error`), refit | $0$ internally; shifted to $\hat\theta$ in `estimand()` | yes (preserved per `para.error` mode) |

**ci.method** controls how the (post-shift) bootstrap distribution is mapped into a CI. Let $B$ be the bootstrap sample of $\hat\theta$, $q_\alpha$ the $\alpha$-quantile of $B$, $\hat\theta$ the point estimate, $\mathrm{SE} = \mathrm{sd}(B)$, $z_\alpha = \Phi^{-1}(\alpha)$, $\alpha = 0.025$ for a 95% CI:

| ci.method | CI formula | symmetry around $\hat\theta$ | bias-aware | skew-aware |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `"normal"` | $\hat\theta \pm z_{1-\alpha} \cdot \mathrm{SE}$ | symmetric | ✗ | ✗ |
| `"basic"` | $[2\hat\theta - q_{1-\alpha},\ 2\hat\theta - q_\alpha]$ | symmetric (reflection) | ✗ | ✗ |
| `"percentile"` | $[q_\alpha,\ q_{1-\alpha}]$ | reflects bootstrap | ✗ | ✗ |
| `"bc"` | bias-shifted percentile, with $z_0 = \Phi^{-1}(P(B < \hat\theta))$ | adapts | ✓ | ✗ |
| `"bca"` | bias + acceleration $a$ from cell-level jackknife | adapts | ✓ | ✓ |

The default ci.method depends on the estimand: `att` → `"normal"`, `att.cumu` → `"basic"`, `aptt` → `"bca"`, `log.att` → `"bca"`. The defaults are calibrated to estimator semantics, not to coverage performance on any specific DGP --- see the empirical coverage study below for the full grid.

**On `"basic"` vs `"percentile"`.** The two reflect different theoretical foundations: `"basic"` inverts the pivot $T = \hat\theta - \theta$ and produces $[2\hat\theta - q_{1-\alpha},\ 2\hat\theta - q_\alpha]$, while `"percentile"` reads the bootstrap quantiles directly as $[q_\alpha,\ q_{1-\alpha}]$. The `"basic"` (or "reflected") interval is the form @davison_hinkley1997 §5.2.1 recommend as the default and is what `boot::boot.ci(type = "basic")` returns; the `"percentile"` interval requires an additional symmetry assumption on the bootstrap distribution to be valid [@hesterberg2014]. When the bootstrap distribution is symmetric the two formulas coincide; on skewed distributions, `"basic"` is more defensible. 

If you want a percentile-based CI as a routine choice, prefer `"basic"` --- it is the literature-standard "percentile" CI in R via the `boot` package. The raw-quantile `"percentile"` option is preserved here mainly for replication of older work and for `boot::boot.ci(type = "perc")` parity.

## Inferential methods in depth

### Unit-level (block) bootstrap

The default is `"bootstrap"`. Resamples whole units with replacement (treated and control pools separately, with rejection sampling to ensure every period is covered) --- so each replicate is a cluster bootstrap with the unit as the cluster. Refits the full model per replicate. The resulting `fit$eff.boot[, , b]` is the per-cell treatment effect estimate from replicate $b$.

This is the most general option. It captures both *unit-level sampling variability* (different treated units have different treatment effects) and *within-unit residual variability* (the noise on each unit's trajectory). For most applied work, this is what you want.

**Caveat.** When treated units are few (say, $N_\mathrm{tr} < 10$), the unit-level bootstrap can degenerate --- some replicates draw a treated pool with a single unit repeated $N_\mathrm{tr}$ times, which gives a near-zero between-unit variance contribution. Switch to `vartype = "jackknife"` in that regime.

### Leave-one-unit-out jackknife

For each unit $i \in 1..N$, refit the model on the remaining $N - 1$ units and record the per-cell treatment effect. The standard error is computed via the Tukey jackknife formula $\sqrt{\frac{N-1}{N} \sum_i \left(\widehat{\mathrm{eff}}_i - \overline{\mathrm{eff}}\right)^2}$. The bootstrap-style storage is $T \times (N-1) \times N$ (each leave-one-out fit drops one column).

Use jackknife when (i) $N_\mathrm{tr}$ is small enough that bootstrap is unstable, or (ii) you want a deterministic SE estimate (no sampling noise from the bootstrap draws). The jackknife SE is asymptotically equivalent to a parametric Wald SE under regularity and is very stable in practice.

**Note:** As of v2.4.2, `estimand()` accepts jackknife fits but restricts `ci.method` to `"normal"`. The other four ci.methods hard-error with a clear message. Jackknife produces an SE estimate via the Tukey pseudo-value formula, not a sampling distribution of $\hat\theta$; `"basic"` / `"percentile"` need exchangeable draws from a sampling distribution (jackknife leave-one-out values are not such draws --- @efron_tibshirani1993 §11; @davison_hinkley1997 §3.2.1) and `"bc"` / `"bca"` need a bootstrap distribution to compute the bias-correction $z_0$ (jackknife has none). Use `ci.method = "normal"` for the Wald-style interval $\hat\theta \pm z \cdot \mathrm{SE}_\mathrm{jack}$, or refit with `vartype = "bootstrap"` for the full ci.method surface.

### Model-based parametric bootstrap

The `"parametric"` path resamples the residual error process and refits the model on each replicate, producing `fit$eff.boot[, , b] = Y.boot - Y0_hat_b` on treated cells. Three residual-error models are supported via the `para.error` argument (sub-option of `vartype = "parametric"`):

| `para.error` | Residual draw | Panel shape | Notes |
|--------------|---------------|-------------|-------|
| `"auto"` (default) | Resolves at fit time | Any | `"empirical"` on a fully-observed panel, `"ar"` on a missing-data panel; `fit$para.error` stores the resolved label |
| `"ar"` | Draw from MVN with AR-vcov estimated from Loop-1 control residuals | Any | The v2.4.1 behavior; universal fallback |
| `"empirical"` | i.i.d. column-resample from main-fit control residuals | Fully observed only | Hard-errors if any cell is missing |
| `"wild"` | Unit-level Rademacher sign-flips over the empirical residual pool [@liu1988; @mammen1993; @cameron_gelbach_miller2008] | Fully observed only | Preserves marginal residual distribution + within-unit dependence; hard-errors if any cell is missing |

The parametric path is the default in the gsynth-style regime (`time.component.from = "nevertreated"`, no treatment reversal, factor-augmented method).

**Caveat.** The simulation is deliberately under $H_0$ (no treatment effect added back to treated cells), which makes `fit$eff.boot` centered at zero. The legacy `fit$est.avg` output and `get.pvalue()` rely on this $H_0$ centering for the $H_0: \mathrm{ATT} = 0$ hypothesis test. In the current implementation, `estimand()` applies a post-hoc location shift `att_b <- att_b - mean(att_b) + estimate` for parametric fits, which centers the bootstrap distribution at the point estimate while preserving its dispersion (`sd()` is invariant to shift). The `"normal"` ci.method is byte-stable across the fix; the other four methods now produce correctly calibrated CIs.

**Conditional-variance target.** The parametric pseudo-treated bootstrap targets the conditional variance $V_t = \mathrm{Var}(\widehat{\mathrm{ATT}}_t - \mathrm{ATT}_t \mid \Lambda, F, X, D)$, holding the treatment indicator $D$ fixed. By the law of total variance, the marginal variance across replications equals $\mathbb{E}_{(\Lambda,F)}[V_t] + \mathrm{Var}_{(\Lambda,F)}[b_t]$ where $b_t$ is the finite-sample conditional bias. Coverage simulations of the parametric path therefore hold $D$ fixed --- re-randomizing $D$ across replications would inject a $\mathrm{Var}_D[b_t]$ term that the bootstrap is silent about, biasing measured coverage downward.

### Parametric bootstrap: valid regimes {#sec-parametric-regimes}

The parametric bootstrap rests on two key assumptions: (a) the time components (factors and loadings) are estimated from a **never-treated** control pool that is independent of the treated units' data, and (b) the model residuals on control units accurately reflect the true error distribution. When either assumption is violated, the parametric bootstrap underestimates standard errors and produces confidence intervals that are too narrow.

To prevent users from invoking the parametric bootstrap in settings where these assumptions fail, the package enforces three hard restrictions:

::: {.callout-important appearance="simple"}
### Three-gate system

| Gate | Condition | What it blocks |
|---|---|---|
| A | `method %in% c("mc", "both")` + `vartype = "parametric"` | matrix completion + parametric |
| B | treatment reversal present + `vartype = "parametric"` | reversal + parametric |
| C | `time.component.from = "notyettreated"` + `vartype = "parametric"` | notyettreated + parametric |

In all three cases the package raises an informative error pointing to the valid alternatives.
:::

The motivation for Gate C comes from both theory and evidence. The `notyettreated` path uses EM imputation to estimate factors jointly across all not-yet-treated observations, including the treated units' pre-period data. EM enlarges the effective factor space by imputing masked treated-post cells, which deflates the observed-cell residuals that the parametric bootstrap feeds into its covariance estimator. In a Monte Carlo study (N = 50, T = 20, r = 2 factors, 500 replications), the blocked `ife + notyettreated + parametric` combination produced **80.6% coverage at nominal 95%** (SE ratio ≈ 0.67). In contrast, all tested nevertreated paths produced near-nominal coverage (0.93–0.96, SE ratio ≈ 1.0). The architectural details of all three gates are documented in `ARCHITECTURE.md`.

If your current code uses `vartype = "parametric"` with the default `time.component.from = "notyettreated"`, migrate as follows:

```r
# Before (now raises an error under Gate C):
fect(data, Y = "Y", D = "D", index = c("id", "time"),
     method = "ife", se = TRUE, vartype = "parametric")

# After --- option 1: switch to nevertreated imputation
# (requires that never-treated control units exist in the data)
fect(data, Y = "Y", D = "D", index = c("id", "time"),
     method = "ife", time.component.from = "nevertreated",
     se = TRUE, vartype = "parametric")

# After --- option 2: use nonparametric bootstrap (safe default)
fect(data, Y = "Y", D = "D", index = c("id", "time"),
     method = "ife", se = TRUE, vartype = "bootstrap")
```

Option 1 switches to the gsynth-style estimation regime and keeps parametric bootstrap. This is appropriate when never-treated control units exist and the treatment does not reverse. Option 2 leaves the estimator unchanged and uses cluster-bootstrap, which is appropriate when the number of treated units is moderate to large. See [Chapter @sec-gsynth] for guidance.

## ci.method choices

`ci.method` decides how the bootstrap distribution becomes a confidence interval. Two layers expose it: `fect()` carries a 2-option `ci.method` for the CIs it ships in `fit$est.att`, `fit$est.avg`, and the placebo / carryover / calendar / cohort / subgroup slots; `estimand()` carries the full 5-option `ci.method` that operates post-hoc on `fit$eff.boot` and `fit$att.avg.unit.boot`. fect's surface is the right entry point for the standard `att` workflow; estimand's surface is the right entry point for the alternative estimands (`att.cumu`, `aptt`, `log.att`).

### `fect()` — `ci.method = c("normal", "basic")`

```r
fect(..., ci.method = "normal")  # default; Wald: theta_hat +- z * SE
fect(..., ci.method = "basic")   # reflected pivot: 2 * theta_hat - quantile(boot, ...)
```

The `"basic"` interval is the reflected (pivot) CI of @davison_hinkley1997 §5.2.1 --- the literature-standard "percentile" CI, and what `boot::boot.ci(type = "basic")` returns in R. Default is `"normal"`. fect's returned `est.att` (per-event-time) and `est.avg` (overall) slots use the requested method and match `estimand(fit, "att", ci.method)` byte-equally on the same fit.

**Parametric fits get a location-shift.** The `vartype = "parametric"` path stores `eff.boot` centered at $0$ (under $H_0$, so the $H_0 = 0$ hypothesis test is correctly calibrated). For `ci.method = "basic"`, the reflected pivot would otherwise collapse around $2\hat\theta$; fect applies the same variance-preserving location shift `eff.boot.ci <- eff.boot - mean(eff.boot) + theta_hat` that `estimand()` applies in `R/po-estimands.R`. The shift is local to the CI computation; the SE and the $H_0$-based $p$-value are unaffected.

**Jackknife rejects basic.** `ci.method = "basic"` on `vartype = "jackknife"` hard-errors with a message pointing the user to `ci.method = "normal"` (the only valid CI for jackknife: $\hat\theta \pm z \cdot \mathrm{SE}_\mathrm{jack}$) or to refitting with `vartype = "bootstrap"`. Jackknife pseudo-values are influence-function-flavored leave-one-out quantities, not exchangeable draws from the sampling distribution [@efron_tibshirani1993 ch11; @davison_hinkley1997 §3.2.1].

**nboots warning.** `ci.method = "basic"` reads the 2.5th / 97.5th order statistics of the bootstrap distribution. These tail quantiles are unstable at small `nboots`; fect emits a warning at fit time when `ci.method = "basic"` is paired with `nboots < 1000`, recommending a refit at `nboots = 1000` for publication-grade CIs [@efron1987 §3; @diciccio_efron1996 §4].


**Other slots.** The location-shift fix is currently applied at the avg-level and per-event-time CI sites only. If your workflow uses `fect()` slots beyond `est.att` and `est.avg` (`est.calendar`, `est.cohort.att`, `est.subgroup.att`, balanced-sample, by-W, placebo, carryover) **and** you fit with `vartype = "parametric"` **and** you request `ci.method = "basic"`, those slots use the legacy reflected formula on the raw $H_0$-centered bootstrap and will produce CIs that don't cover correctly. Until those slots are migrated, call `estimand(fit, type, by, ci.method = "basic")` for the slot you need.

The legacy `quantile.CI` argument is **soft-deprecated** as of v2.4.2: an explicit `quantile.CI = FALSE` is now equivalent to `ci.method = "normal"`, and `quantile.CI = TRUE` is equivalent to `ci.method = "basic"`. Both still work but emit a one-time deprecation warning.

### `estimand()` — `ci.method = c("normal", "basic", "percentile", "bc", "bca")`

`estimand()` exposes the full 5-method surface for post-hoc CI construction on the same fit. None of the methods add per-replicate cost --- they are post-hoc operations on the already-stored `eff.boot` surface (`bca` adds a cheap cell-level jackknife with no extra refits).

| `ci.method` | Formula | When to prefer |
|-------------|---------|----------------|
| `"normal"` | $\hat\theta \pm z \cdot \mathrm{SE}$ (Wald) | Symmetric distributions; the per-type default for `att` |
| `"basic"` | $(2\hat\theta - q_{1-\alpha/2},\ 2\hat\theta - q_{\alpha/2})$ | Reflected pivot CI [@davison_hinkley1997 §5.2.1; `boot::boot.ci(type = "basic")`]; the per-type default for `att.cumu` |
| `"percentile"` | $(q_{\alpha/2},\ q_{1-\alpha/2})$ | Raw bootstrap quantiles; preserved for replication and `boot::boot.ci(type = "perc")` parity --- prefer `"basic"` for routine work |
| `"bc"` | Bias-corrected percentile [@efron1987 minus acceleration]; cutoffs shifted by $2 z_0$ | Skewed distributions where the bootstrap median is biased |
| `"bca"` (new in v2.4.2) | Bias-corrected accelerated [@efron1987 in full]; adds an acceleration parameter via cell-level jackknife | Skewed *and* heavy-tailed; the per-type default for `aptt` and `log.att` |

`ci.method` defaults to `NULL` in `estimand()`, which triggers a per-type default: `att` → `"normal"`, `att.cumu` → `"basic"`, `aptt` → `"bca"`, `log.att` → `"bca"`. Pass an explicit value to override.

For tail-quantile-based methods (`"basic"`, `"percentile"`, `"bc"`, `"bca"`), `estimand()` warns when the fit has fewer than 1000 bootstrap replicates and recommends a refit at `nboots = 1000` for stable tail estimation [@efron1987 §3; @diciccio_efron1996 §4].

### When the two surfaces overlap

`fect(ci.method = "normal")` and `estimand(fit, type = "att", ci.method = "normal")` produce identical numerics on the avg slot (within RNG noise from independent fits, or byte-identical when the two operate on the same `fit$eff.boot`). Same for `"basic"`. The two-surface design exists because:

1. fect's `est.*` slots are populated at fit time; users want a CI in the printed output without having to call `estimand()` separately. The simple normal/basic dichotomy covers the routine `att` workflow.
2. estimand's full 5-method surface is needed for the alternative estimands (`att.cumu`, `aptt`, `log.att`) where the bootstrap distribution is skewed and bias-correction (`bc`, `bca`) materially changes the CI.

A user who wants `bca` on the standard `att` slot should fit with the default `ci.method` and call `estimand(fit, type = "att", ci.method = "bca")` post-fit. fect() rejects `ci.method = "bca"` (and `"bc"`, `"percentile"`) with an error pointing to estimand().

## p-values via test inversion

Each `ci.method` corresponds to a $p$-value formula obtained by inverting the CI procedure: the two-sided $p$-value for testing $H_0: \theta = \theta_0$ is the smallest $\alpha$ at which the $(1 - \alpha)$ CI excludes $\theta_0$.

For the standard test of $H_0: \theta = 0$ (the no-effect null):

| ci.method | $p$-value |
|---|---|
| `"normal"` | $2 (1 - \Phi(|\hat\theta| / \mathrm{SE}))$ |
| `"percentile"` | $2 \min(P(B \le 0),\ P(B \ge 0))$ |
| `"basic"` | $2 \min(P(B \ge 2\hat\theta),\ P(B \le 2\hat\theta))$ |
| `"bc"` | $2 \Phi\!\left(z_0 - |\Phi^{-1}(P(B \le 0)) - z_0|\right)$ where $z_0 = \Phi^{-1}(P(B < \hat\theta))$ |
| `"bca"` | bca formula with $z_0$ and acceleration $a$ (skewness correction) |

The legacy `fit$est.avg` $p$-value (in the `p.value` column of the `fit$est.att` matrix) uses the `"normal"` formula by default, or the `"percentile"` formula when `quantile.CI = TRUE`. The current `estimand()` API does *not* yet emit a `p.value` column; users who need a non-`"normal"` $p$-value can compute it from `fit$eff.boot` using the formulas above. A `p.value` column is planned for a future release.

For parametric vartype, the `get.pvalue()` two-sided $p$-value is computed on the $H_0$-centered bootstrap distribution, not the shifted ($H_1$-centered) distribution --- this is correct, because that $p$-value tests $H_0: \mathrm{ATT} = 0$, which is a hypothesis-test question that the $H_0$ simulation answers directly. The location-shift fix in `estimand()` does not propagate to `get.pvalue()` for this reason.

## How many bootstrap replicates?

The classic literature gives different recommendations by ci.method, driven by what the method needs from the bootstrap distribution:

| ci.method | quantity needed | $B$ minimum | $B$ preferred | reference |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `"normal"` | $\mathrm{sd}(B)$ | 50 | 200 | @efron_tibshirani1993 §12 |
| `"percentile"` | tail quantiles $q_{2.5}, q_{97.5}$ | 1,000 | 2,000 | @efron_tibshirani1993 §13.3; @davison_hinkley1997 §5.3.1 |
| `"basic"` | tail quantiles | 1,000 | 2,000 | same |
| `"bc"` | quantiles + bias correction $z_0$ | 1,000 | 2,000 | @efron1987 |
| `"bca"` | quantiles + $z_0$ + acceleration $a$ | 1,000 | 2,000+ | @diciccio_efron1996; @hesterberg2014 |

The fect default is `nboots = 200`, calibrated for the SE-based normal CI (which is the per-type default for `att`, the most common estimand). The simulation evidence below shows that 200 replicates is enough for nominal coverage on `att` across all four scenarios under `ci.method = "normal"` --- the SE estimate stabilizes well before the tail quantiles do. For tail-quantile CI methods (`basic`, `percentile`, `bc`, `bca`), the literature minimum of 1,000 is more accurate; the simulation evidence shows that on a small-Ntr cluster bootstrap (Scenario C1 below), tail-CI coverage drifts to 0.92-0.93 at `nboots = 200` and recovers to 0.94-0.945 at `nboots = 1000`. For BCa CIs reported as the primary inferential output of a paper, `nboots = 2000` is the canonical recommendation.

The `estimand()` warning gate `.check_tail_ci_replicates` fires when a tail-CI method is requested on a fit with `nboots < 1000`; the warning text gives the literature citation and points users to refit with `nboots = 1000`.

## Empirical coverage

The minimal coverage suite (`tests/coverage-study/`) exercises the v2.4.2 inference paths at $K = 200$ replications on three scenarios:

- **A** --- factor model $r = 2$, IID errors, `vartype = "parametric"`. $N_{tr} = 5$, $N_{co} = 50$, $T = 30$, $T_0 = 20$.
- **B** --- same DGP and estimator as A, but errors are AR(1) with $\rho = 0.8$.
- **C** --- additive TWFE ($r = 0$), AR(1) errors with $\rho = 0.5$, $N_{tr} = 20$, $N_{co} = 80$. **C₁** uses `vartype = "bootstrap"`; **C₂** uses `vartype = "jackknife"`.

Full DGP / estimator settings are in [`tests/coverage-study/results/README.md`](https://github.com/xuyiqing/fect/blob/main/tests/coverage-study/results/README.md). Coverage target for A and B is the realized average treated-post effect within each rep; for C, the population $\mathrm{ATT} = 3$.

### `fect()` ci.method surface

`fit$est.avg` coverage at $K = 200$, $\mathrm{nboots} = 200$:

| scenario | ci.method | coverage | Monte Carlo SE | mean width |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|
| A | normal | 0.960 | 0.014 | 0.800 |
| A | basic  | 0.960 | 0.014 | 0.780 |
| B | normal | 0.960 | 0.014 | 1.724 |
| B | basic  | 0.950 | 0.015 | 1.690 |
| C₁ | normal | 0.945 | 0.016 | 0.583 |
| C₁ | basic  | 0.935 | 0.017 | 0.571 |
| C₂ | normal | 0.930 | 0.018 | 0.605 |

All cells nominal within Monte Carlo noise.

### `estimand()` ci.method surface

Same fits, post-hoc `estimand(fit, "att", "overall", ci.method)` at $\mathrm{nboots} = 200$:

| scenario | ci.method | coverage | Monte Carlo SE | mean width |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|
| A | normal     | 0.960 | 0.014 | 0.800 |
| A | basic      | 0.960 | 0.014 | 0.780 |
| A | percentile | 0.935 | 0.017 | 0.780 |
| A | bc         | 0.935 | 0.017 | 0.786 |
| A | bca        | 0.940 | 0.017 | 0.786 |
| B | normal     | 0.960 | 0.014 | 1.724 |
| B | basic      | 0.950 | 0.015 | 1.690 |
| B | percentile | 0.960 | 0.014 | 1.690 |
| B | bc         | 0.960 | 0.014 | 1.694 |
| B | bca        | 0.960 | 0.014 | 1.694 |
| C₁ | normal     | 0.945 | 0.016 | 0.583 |
| C₁ | basic      | 0.935 | 0.017 | 0.571 |
| C₁ | percentile | 0.925 | 0.019 | 0.571 |
| C₁ | bc         | 0.920 | 0.019 | 0.568 |
| C₁ | bca        | 0.920 | 0.019 | 0.568 |
| C₂ | normal     | 0.930 | 0.018 | 0.605 |

A, B, and C₂ are at nominal across all ci.methods. In C₁, the tail-quantile methods (`basic`, `percentile`, `bc`, `bca`) come in at 0.92-0.935 at $\mathrm{nboots} = 200$; at $\mathrm{nboots} = 1000$ they recover to 0.94-0.945, the operating point @efron1987 §3 and @diciccio_efron1996 §4 recommend.

### Calibration

Calibration ratios (mean SE / empirical SD across reps) by scenario: A = 1.05, B = 1.04, C₁ = 1.01, C₂ = 1.05. The procedures correctly calibrate variance to within 5 % in every cell; any coverage slack is finite-sample and resolves at higher $K$ or $B$.

## Decision tree

A practical default when picking a (vartype, ci.method, para.error) combination:

```text
Is the design factor-augmented with never-treated controls, no
reversal, and using `method` in {"gsynth", "ife", "cfe"}?

├── Yes: vartype = "parametric", para.error = "auto"
│        para.error = "auto" resolves to "empirical" on a fully-
│          observed panel and to "ar" when missing-data cells exist
│        Override para.error to "wild" or "empirical" only when you
│          have a fully-observed panel and a reason to bypass the
│          resolved default
│        Use normal for att, bca for aptt / log.att

└── No: choose between bootstrap and jackknife by treated-unit count

    ├── Ntr small (< 10): vartype = "jackknife"
    │        ci.method = "normal" (the only ci.method jackknife accepts)
    │        Wald-style interval theta-hat ± z * SE_jack

    └── Ntr >= 10: vartype = "bootstrap" (unit-level cluster bootstrap)
                    Use normal for att, bca for aptt / log.att
                    Bump nboots to 1000+ when using a tail-quantile
                      ci.method (basic / percentile / bc / bca)
```
